name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
۞ أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا۟ مِن دِيَـٰرِهِمْ وَهُمْ أُلُوفٌ حَذَرَ ٱلْمَوْتِ فَقَالَ لَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مُوتُوا۟ ثُمَّ أَحْيَـٰهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٢٤٣)
```

```english
Have you not regarded those who left their homes in thousands, apprehensive of death, whereupon Allah said to them, ‘Die,’ then He revived them? Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind, but most people do not give thanks. (243)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ خَرَجُوا مِن دِيَارِهِمْ وَهُمْ أُلُوفٌ حَذَرَ الْمَوْتِ** "Have you not regarded those who left their homes in thousands, precarious of death?" Seeing (الرؤية) here carries the meaning of knowledge (العلم). This expression is employed to claim that the matter is so manifest that knowledge of it is regarded as seeing it. It is similar to His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ اللَّهَ خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ** "Have you not regarded that Allah created the heavens and the earth with the truth?" (Ibrahim: 19), and His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ تَرَوْا كَيْفَ خَلَقَ اللَّهُ سَبْعَ سَمَاوَاتٍ طِبَاقًا** "Have you not regarded how Allah created the seven heavens in layers?" (Nuh: 15).

Al-Zamakhshari mentioned that the phrase "Have you not regarded" (ألم تر) functions like a proverb; it is invoked in contexts of wonderment. Thus, our saying "Have you not seen such and such" means "Are you not amazed by such and such?" The phrase "precarious of death" (حذر الموت) is an object of purpose (مفعول له), though it is also possible for it to be an absolute object (مفعول مطلق), with the implied meaning being "they feared death with a great fearing."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَقَالَ لَهُمُ اللَّهُ مُوتُوا ثُمَّ أَحْيَاهُمْ** "whereupon Allah said to them, ‘Die!’ Then He revived them." The command (الأمر) here is existential (تكويني) — *pertaining to the divine decree that brings things into being* — and it does not contradict the fact that their death occurred through a natural course, as mentioned in the narrations that it was by means of a plague. The use of the command, rather than saying "Allah caused them to die and then revived them," is intended to be more indicative of the effective penetration of Divine Power and the dominance of His command. Indeed, expression through the creative imperative (الإنشاء) in existential matters (التكوينيات) is more potent and emphatic than expression through reporting (الإخبار). Conversely, expression in the form of reporting to indicate occurrence in legislative matters (التشريعيات) is more potent and emphatic than the imperative. Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted, "Then He revived them" (ثم أحياهم) is not devoid of the indication that Allah revived them so that they might live; thus, they lived on after their revival. Had their revival been merely for a lesson to be learned by others, or to complete a proof, or to clarify a reality, it would have been mentioned as such, according to the custom of the Qur'an in its eloquence, as seen in the story of the People of the Cave. Moreover, His saying, the Exalted, immediately after: "Indeed Allah is gracious to mankind," also intimates this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ** "but most people do not give thanks." The use of the explicit noun in place of a pronoun—namely, the repetition of the word "mankind" (الناس) for the second time—is due to its indication of the low level of their intellects. Furthermore, those upon whom Allah bestowed the favor of revival were a specific group, and the meaning is not that the majority of that specific group were ungrateful, but rather the majority of all mankind. This verse is not without some relevance to the verses that follow it concerning the obligation of fighting (القتال), as there is in struggle (الجهاد) a revival of the nation (الملة) after its death.

Some commentators have mentioned that the verse is a parable (مثل) struck by Allah for the state of a nation in its decline and death through the humiliation imposed by foreigners who spread their authority over it, and then its life through its uprising and defense of its vital rights and independence in self-governance.

He said, in summary: If the verse were intended to relate a story from the stories of the Children of Israel—as most narrations indicate—or others, it would have been necessary to point out that they were from the Children of Israel and to mention the prophet who revived them, as is the custom of the Qur'an in its other stories. Yet the verse is devoid of that. Furthermore, the Torah does not mention this in the stories of the Prophet Ezekiel (peace be upon him and upon our Prophet and his Progeny). Thus, the narrations are nothing but *Isra'iliyyat* (إسرائيليات) — *narratives of Judeo-Christian origin* — which the Jews interpolated. Moreover, worldly death and life are but one death and one life, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **لَا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا الْمَوْتَ إِلَّا الْمَوْتَةَ الْأُولَىٰ** "They will not taste death therein except for the first death" (Ad-Dukhan: 56), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ** "and You have given us life twice" (Al-Mu'min: 11). Therefore, there is no meaning for two lives in this world. Thus, the verse is presented as a parable, and what is meant by it is a people who were attacked by those of power and strength among their enemies, who sought to humiliate and subjugate them, spreading authority over them and ruling them. They did not defend their independence and left their homes while they were thousands—possessing numbers and resolve—precarious of death. So Allah said to them, "Die the death of disgrace and ignorance," for ignorance and lethargy are death, just as knowledge and the refusal of injustice are life. The Exalted said: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذَا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيكُمْ** "O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life" (Al-Anfal: 24), and the Exalted said: **أَوَمَن كَانَ مَيْتًا فَأَحْيَيْنَاهُ وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُ نُورًا يَمْشِي بِهِ فِي النَّاسِ كَمَن مَّثَلُهُ فِي الظُّلُمَاتِ لَيْسَ بِخَارِجٍ مِّنْهَا** "Is he who was lifeless, then We gave him life and maintained for him a light by which he walks among the people, like one whose description is that he is in a manifold darkness from which he cannot emerge?" (Al-An'am: 122).

In short, these people die through disgrace and the empowerment of enemies over them, and they remain dead. Then Allah revived them by casting into them the spirit of uprising and the defense of truth. Thus, they stood up for their rights and became independent in their affairs. These people whom Allah revived, even if they are different individuals from those whom Allah caused to die, the whole is one nation that died at one time and lived at another time later on. Allah has regarded a people as one despite the difference in individuals, as in His saying to the Children of Israel: **أَنجَيْنَاكُم مِّنْ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ** "We delivered you from Pharaoh’s clan" (Al-A'raf: 141), and His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَاكُم مِّن بَعْدِ مَوْتِكُمْ** "Then We raised you up after your death" (Al-Baqarah: 56). Were it not for what we mentioned regarding the verse being presented as a parable, the connection of the verse to the verses of fighting that follow it would not be sound, and this is evident. This is the summary of what he mentioned.

This discourse, as you see, is based **firstly** on the denial of miracles (المعجزات) and the breaking of natural norms (خوارق العادات) — *supernatural events that transcend the regular laws of nature* — or some of them, such as the revival of the dead. The proof for these has already passed. Furthermore, the manifest sense of the Qur'an in establishing the breaking of norms through the revival of the dead and the like is something that cannot be denied, even if we were unable to prove its possibility through the way of reason (العقل).

**Secondly:** It is based on the claim that the Qur'an indicates the impossibility of more than one life in this world, as he argued with verses like His saying, the Exalted: **لَا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا الْمَوْتَ إِلَّا الْمَوْتَةَ الْأُولَىٰ** "They will not taste death therein except for the first death" (Ad-Dukhan: 56), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَحْيَيْتَنَا اثْنَتَيْنِ** "and You have given us life twice" (Al-Mu'min: 11).

The response to this is that all the verses indicating the revival of the dead—as in the stories of Ibrahim, Musa, 'Isa, and 'Uzayr—in a manner whose indication cannot be repelled, are sufficient to refute what he mentioned. Moreover, worldly life does not become two lives by the intervention of death, as is most clearly understood from the story of 'Uzayr, where he did not perceive his prolonged death. What is meant by the verses he cited is the type of life.

**Thirdly:** It is based on the idea that if the verse were intended to relate a story, it would have identified their people and specified the prophet who revived them.

You know that the methods of eloquence (البلاغة) are diverse and varied. Just as speech may follow the course of prolixity (الإطناب), it may also follow the course of brevity (الإيجاز). The verse has parallels in the Qur'an, such as His saying, the Exalted: **قُتِلَ أَصْحَابُ الْأُخْدُودِ النَّارِ ذَاتِ الْوَقُودِ إِذْ هُمْ عَلَيْهَا قُعُودٌ وَهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَا يَفْعَلُونَ بِالْمُؤْمِنِينَ شُهُودٌ** "Perish the Men of the Ditch! The fire abounding in fuel, as they sat by it, and were witnesses to what they did to the faithful" (Al-Buruj: 4-7), and His saying, the Exalted: **وَمِمَّنْ خَلَقْنَا أُمَّةٌ يَهْدُونَ بِالْحَقِّ وَبِهِ يَعْدِلُونَ** "Among those We have created are a nation who guide by the truth and do justice thereby" (Al-A'raf: 181).

**Fourthly:** It is based on the claim that if the verse is not taken as a parable, it would not be connected in meaning to the verses following it. You know that the gradual revelation of the Qur'an (نجوماً) renders unnecessary any forced attempt to link verses to one another, except for those whose connection is manifest, being joined according to the requirements of eloquent speech.

The truth is that the verse, as is its manifest sense, is intended to relate a story. I wonder, what eloquence is there in Allah, the Glorified, delivering to people a speech in which most observers see nothing but a story from the stories of the past, while it is in reality a parable based on imagination (التخييل) without reality?

This is especially so when the custom of His speech, the Exalted, is to distinguish a parable from other things in all the parables placed within it, through phrases such as: **مَثَلُهُمْ كَمَثَلِ الَّذِي** "Their parable is that of one who..." (Al-Baqarah: 17), and His saying: **إِنَّمَا مَثَلُ الْحَيَاةِ الدُّنْيَا** "The parable of the life of this world is only..." (Yunus: 24), and His saying: **مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا** "The parable of those who were charged..." (Al-Jumu'ah: 5), and others.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Ihtijaj*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he (peace be upon him) said: **أحيى الله قوماً خرجوا من اوطانهم هاربين من الطاعون ، لا يحصى عددهم ، فأماتهم الله دهراً طويلاً حتى بليت عظامهم ، وتقطعت أوصالهم ، وصاروا تراباً ، فبعث الله في وقت احب ان يرى خلقه نبياً يقال له : حزقيل ، فدعاهم فاجتمعت ابدانهم ، ورجعت فيها ارواحهم ، وقاموا كهيئة يوم ماتوا ، لا يفتقدون في اعدادهم رجلاً فعاشوا بعد ذلك دهراً طويلاً .** — "Allah revived a people who left their homelands fleeing from the plague; their number could not be counted. So Allah caused them to die for a long period until their bones decayed, their joints separated, and they became dust. Then, at a time when He wished to show His creation, Allah sent a prophet called Ezekiel (حزقيل). He called them, and their bodies gathered, their spirits returned to them, and they stood up in the same state as the day they died, not missing a single man from their number. They lived after that for a long time."

I say: This meaning was narrated by al-Kulayni and al-'Ayyashi in a more detailed manner, and at its end: "And concerning them, this verse was revealed."
